title: काशी पञ्चकम्
devanagariText:
॥ श्री काशी पञ्चकम् ॥

मनो निवृत्तिः परमोपशान्तिः
सा तीर्थवर्या मणिकर्णिका च।
ज्ञानप्रवाहा विमलादिगङ्गा
सा काशिकाहं निजबोधरूपा॥ १॥

यस्यामिदं कल्पितमिन्द्रजालं
चराचरं भाति मनोविलासम्।
सा काशिकाहं निजबोधरूपा
विश्वेश्वरोऽस्मीति विभावयेत्सः॥ २॥

कोशेषु पञ्चस्वधिराजमाना
बुद्धिर्भवानी प्रतिदेहगेहम्।
साक्षी शिवः सर्वगतोऽन्तरात्मा
सा काशिकाहं निजबोधरूपा॥ ३॥

काश्यां हि काशते काशी
काशी सर्वप्रकाशिका।
सा काशी विदिता येन
तेन प्राप्ता हि काशिका॥ ४॥

काशीक्षेत्रं शरीरं त्रिभुवनजननी
व्यापिनी ज्ञानगङ्गा
भक्तिर्विश्वेश्वरस्य श्रवणमननयोः
संयमो ध्यानयोगः।
विश्वेशोऽयं तुरीयः सकलजनमनः-
साक्षिभूतोऽन्तरात्मा
देहे सर्वं मदीयं यदिह हि काश्यां
नास्ति मे तद्विहाय॥ ५॥

॥ इति श्रीमच्छंकराचार्यविरचितम् काशीपञ्चकम् सम्पूर्णम् ॥
transliteration:
|| Shri Kashi Panchakam ||

Mano-nivrittih parama-upashantih
Sa tirtha-varya Manikarnika cha |
Jnana-pravaha vimala-adi-Ganga
Sa Kashika-aham nija-bodha-rupa || 1 ||

Yasyam-idam kalpitam-indra-jalam
Characharam bhati mano-vilasam |
Sa Kashika-aham nija-bodha-rupa
Vishveshvaro-asmi-iti vibhavayet-sah || 2 ||

Kosheshu panchasu-adhirajamana
Buddhir-Bhavani prati-deha-geham |
Sakshi Shivah sarva-gato-antaratma
Sa Kashika-aham nija-bodha-rupa || 3 ||

Kashyam hi kashate Kashi
Kashi sarva-prakashika |
Sa Kashi vidita yena
Tena prapta hi Kashika || 4 ||

Kashi-kshetram shariram tri-bhuvana-janani
Vyapini jnana-Ganga
Bhaktir-Vishveshvarasya shravana-manana-yoh
Samyamo dhyana-yogah |
Vishvesho-ayam turiyah sakala-jana-manah-
Sakshi-bhuto-antaratma
Dehe sarvam madiyam yad-iha hi Kashyam
Nasti me tad-vihaya || 5 ||

|| Iti Shrimach-Shankaracharya-virachitam Kashi-Panchakam Sampurnam ||
hindiMeaning:
मन की निवृत्ति परम उपशान्ति है — वही श्रेष्ठ तीर्थ मणिकर्णिका है। ज्ञान का प्रवाह ही विमल गंगा है — वही काशी है, मैं निजबोधरूप हूँ॥ १॥

जिसमें यह चराचर इन्द्रजाल-सा मनोविलास भासता है — वही काशी मैं निजबोधरूप हूँ — 'मैं विश्वेश्वर हूँ' ऐसा भावन करे॥ २॥

पाँच कोशों में अधिराज बुद्धि ही भवानी है, प्रत्येक देह-गृह में। साक्षी शिव सर्वगत अन्तरात्मा हैं — वही काशी है, निजबोधरूप॥ ३॥

काशी में काशी प्रकाशित होती है — काशी सबको प्रकाशित करने वाली है। जिसने काशी को जान लिया, उसने काशी प्राप्त कर ली॥ ४॥

काशी क्षेत्र यह शरीर है, त्रिभुवनजननी व्यापिनी ज्ञानगंगा है, विश्वेश्वर की भक्ति श्रवण-मनन है, संयम ध्यानयोग है। विश्वेश्वर तुरीय है — सकल जनमनों का साक्षी अन्तरात्मा। देह में सब मेरा है — जो काशी में है वही सब है, उसके बिना कुछ नहीं॥ ५॥
benefits: Reveals Kashi as the inner spiritual state — not just a place
Teaches that the body itself is the sacred Kashi Kshetra
Identifies Ganga as the flow of knowledge within
Invokes liberation through understanding vishweshwara as antaratma
Supreme Advaita teaching connecting pilgrimage to self-realization
Ideal for seekers who cannot visit Kashi physically
description: The Kashi Panchakam, known in Sanskrit as काशी पञ्चकम्, is a set of five devotional verses in praise of Lord Shiva. Drawn from Kashi Panchakam — Adi Shankaracharya, this text has been cherished by devotees for centuries. Regular recitation is believed to reveal Kashi as the inner spiritual state — not just a place, grant liberation through understanding Vishweshwara as Antaratma, and identify Ganga as the flow of knowledge within. It is especially recommended on Monday (Somvar) and during Maha Shivaratri and Shravan Somvar for maximum spiritual benefit. The text contains 5 verses and takes around 5 minutes to recite, fitting conveniently into a daily spiritual routine. On this page, you can read the complete Kashi Panchakam in Devanagari Sanskrit with English transliteration, Hindi meaning (arth), and free PDF download for offline recitation.

seoFaqs.question: What is the Kashi Panchakam and who composed it?
seoFaqs.answer: The Kashi Panchakam (काशी पञ्चकम्) is a celebrated five-verse Advaita stotra composed by Jagadguru Adi Shankaracharya, the great 8th-century preceptor of Advaita Vedanta. As its colophon declares — Iti Shrimach-Shankaracharya-virachitam Kashi-Panchakam Sampurnam — the hymn is preserved across centuries in the classical Shankara tradition. Unlike conventional pilgrimage stotras that praise the outer city of Varanasi, the Kashi Panchakam is a profound non-dual teaching that identifies Kashi as the inner state of pure self-knowledge — declaring 'Sa Kashika-aham nija-bodha-rupa,' 'I myself am that Kashi, of the very nature of inner awareness.' It is recited by Advaita sadhakas, sannyasis of the Dashanami order, and devotees of Vishweshwar across Smarta tradition. (Sources: Adi Shankaracharya stotra corpus; classical Shankara tradition.)

seoFaqs.question: What does the refrain 'Sa Kashika-aham nija-bodha-rupa' mean?
seoFaqs.answer: The refrain Sa Kashika-aham nija-bodha-rupa concludes the first three verses of the Kashi Panchakam and carries the entire Advaitic essence of the hymn. Sa — 'that' (the Kashi just described); Kashika — 'the city of Kashi, the illuminator'; aham — 'I am'; nija-bodha-rupa — 'of the form of my own innate awareness/consciousness.' Together: 'That Kashi I myself am, being of the very nature of pure inner consciousness.' The verse turns the entire pilgrimage paradigm inward — Manikarnika is the cessation of the mind, the Ganga is the flow of jnana, the city of Kashi is the body, and Vishweshwar is the antaratma, the witnessing Self. Shankara teaches that the true Kashi-yatra is not a journey to a place but the recognition of one's own bodha-svarupa. (Sources: Kashi Panchakam verses 1-3; classical Advaita commentary tradition.)

seoFaqs.question: What is the meaning of the first verse 'Mano nivrittih parama-upashantih'?
seoFaqs.answer: The opening verse — Mano-nivrittih parama-upashantih sa tirtha-varya Manikarnika cha, jnana-pravaha vimala-adi-Ganga sa Kashika-aham nija-bodha-rupa — establishes the entire Advaitic framework. Mano-nivrittih (the cessation of mental modifications) is parama-upashantih (the supreme peace), and that very state is sa tirtha-varya Manikarnika — the supreme tirtha called Manikarnika. The continuous flow of jnana (jnana-pravaha) is itself the pure primordial Ganga (vimala-adi-Ganga). Therefore the inner Kashi — where mind ceases, supreme peace dawns, and knowledge flows like the Ganga — is none other than one's own self of pure awareness. The verse compresses the entire Vedanta into four lines. (Sources: Kashi Panchakam verse 1; classical Advaita commentary.)

seoFaqs.question: What is the spiritual significance of the body as Kashi Kshetra in verse 5?
seoFaqs.answer: The fifth verse declares Kashi-kshetram shariram — 'the body itself is the Kashi Kshetra.' This is one of the most powerful Advaitic teachings in the Shankara stotra corpus. The all-pervading jnana-Ganga is the tribhuvana-janani (mother of the three worlds) flowing within; bhakti to Vishweshwar takes the form of shravana and manana; samyama (sense-restraint) is dhyana-yoga; Vishweshwar himself is the turiya — the fourth state of consciousness, the antaratma who is the witness of all minds. The seeker therefore recognises that whatever exists in the outer Kashi exists fully within his own body-temple, and that nothing is missing for one who has realised this inner pilgrimage. The verse offers profound consolation to devotees who cannot physically reach Varanasi. (Sources: Kashi Panchakam verse 5; Shankara Advaita tradition.)

seoFaqs.question: How and when should the Kashi Panchakam be recited?
seoFaqs.answer: The Kashi Panchakam is most powerful when recited in Brahma muhurta (pre-dawn) after bath, on a clean asana, facing east or north, with mind concentrated on the meaning of each verse rather than mere mechanical chanting. It is especially auspicious on Mondays (Somvar — sacred to Shiva-Vishweshwar), every Pradosh tithi, Maha Shivaratri (Phalguna Krishna Chaturdashi), throughout the month of Shravan and on every Shravan Somvar, on Kartik Purnima (Dev Diwali at Kashi), and as part of the daily nityapaath of Advaita sadhakas. Devotees who cannot travel to Varanasi often recite it as a substitute Kashi-yatra, holding the inner meaning that the body itself is the sacred kshetra. (Sources: Shankara stotra paddhati; classical Advaita recitation tradition.)

seoFaqs.question: What are the benefits of reciting the Kashi Panchakam?
seoFaqs.answer: Traditional Advaita teachers attribute the following benefits to regular recitation of the Kashi Panchakam with understanding: realization of the inner Kashi as one's own bodha-svarupa, freedom from the misconception that liberation depends on outer pilgrimage alone, awakening of the discrimination (viveka) that the body is the true kshetra, recognition of Vishweshwar as the antaratma witnessing all experience, the supreme phala of Kashi-vasa (residence in Kashi) attained through inner contemplation rather than physical travel, peace of mind through mano-nivritti, and ultimately moksha as taught in the closing benediction. The hymn is especially recommended for sannyasis, Advaita sadhakas and devotees of Vishweshwar living far from Varanasi. (Sources: classical Shankara phalashruti; Advaita teaching tradition.)
